primary_diagnosis.name: Acute health-anxiety / panic reaction triggered by misinterpreting an incidental positive parietal-cell antibody screening result, on a background of early (asymptomatic) autoimmune atrophic gastritis

comorbidities.name: Early autoimmune (metaplastic) atrophic gastritis — asymptomatic, screening-detected
Mildly reduced vitamin B12 absorption (no anaemia reported)

pathophysiology_summary: The presenting problem is an acute autonomic (sympathetic) arousal — a panic reaction — precipitated by health anxiety after the patient read and catastrophically misinterpreted an incidental positive parietal-cell antibody result while researching gastric cancer online. Surges of catecholamines produce the tachycardia, palpitations, nausea, epigastric tightening, hyperventilation and sense of impending doom; these are physiological anxiety symptoms, not signs of haemorrhage or ischaemia. The underlying incidental finding — parietal-cell antibodies — reflects early autoimmune atrophic gastritis, a slowly progressive, typically asymptomatic thinning of the gastric body mucosa over years to decades that can gradually reduce intrinsic-factor-dependent B12 absorption (and, long-term, carries a modestly increased gastric neoplasia risk warranting routine surveillance) but is entirely benign in the acute setting. The clinical task is to exclude the dangerous mimics (upper-GI haemorrhage, acute coronary syndrome) by history, deliver calibrated reassurance and anxiety de-escalation, and arrange routine GP follow-up for B12 monitoring — while safety-netting firmly for true bleed/cardiac red flags.

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden onset immediately after a psychological trigger (reading and researching an abnormal result), with autonomic symptoms and a catastrophic cognitive appraisal.
Patient fears internal bleeding and reports epigastric symptoms; must be actively excluded by asking about haematemesis, melena and syncope before reassurance.
Palpitations, breathlessness and nausea can be an atypical cardiac presentation; must be screened rather than assumed anxiety.
Upper-abdominal discomfort raises PUD, but there is no localised point tenderness and no NSAID history.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the two emergency mimics (GI bleed, ACS) must remain live until excluded by targeted history — anchoring prematurely on anxiety is a premature-closure error.

differential.evidence_basis: Denies haematemesis, melena and syncope; symptoms track the psychological trigger and the timeline — the constellation is a panic response, not organ pathology.
No haematemesis, no melena, no syncope or postural symptoms — no clinical evidence of an active bleed on history.
Lower probability given the clear anxiety trigger and absence of exertional ischaemic-type chest pain, but cardiac symptoms warrant a low threshold to escalate if they change.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a clean red-flag screen the leading diagnosis is anxiety/panic; the residual not-to-miss item is cardiac, handled by safety-netting rather than emergency workup in a low-risk patient.

differential.diagnosis: Acute health-anxiety / panic reaction to an incidental autoimmune-gastritis screening result
differential.evidence_basis: Benign autonomic arousal from misinterpreting a non-urgent finding; managed with reassurance, grounding and routine GP follow-up, with firm red-flag safety-netting.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The autoimmune gastritis itself is an incidental, non-acute finding requiring routine surveillance — not the cause of tonight's symptoms.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate an active upper-GI bleed requiring emergency assessment; its absence is a key discriminator against the patient's feared diagnosis.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A true upper-GI bleed reassured away could decompensate; conversely, failing to ask leaves the emergency mimic unexcluded.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Signals a significant upper-GI bleed; absence supports a benign course.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed melena can mean a large occult bleed presenting late with collapse.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Would suggest haemodynamic compromise from blood loss or a cardiac cause.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed haemodynamic instability could progress to shock.

red_flags.description: Ischaemic-type chest pain (crushing/exertional, radiating), or breathlessness not settling with de-escalation
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would raise acute coronary syndrome as an atypical cardiac presentation rather than anxiety.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Anchoring on anxiety and missing an atypical MI.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Would raise a perforated viscus or vascular catastrophe requiring emergency care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A catastrophic abdominal event mistaken for anxiety.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Labelling the whole picture as a panic attack from the opening line without first screening for haematemesis, melena, syncope and cardiac symptoms — the emergency mimics must be actively excluded, not assumed away.

Being pulled by the patient's fear (and the abnormal-looking lab) into an unnecessary ED referral or emergency cardiac/GI workup, reinforcing health anxiety and causing an avoidable ED surge for a benign anxiety response.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Misreading an incidental finding as urgent
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating parietal-cell antibodies / early autoimmune gastritis as an acute or sinister problem rather than a slowly progressive, routinely-surveilled incidental finding.
Swinging the other way and dismissing the patient as merely anxious without validating distress or providing firm red-flag safety-netting, so a genuine future bleed or cardiac event is not acted on.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Blood pressure (clinician-measured 142/88 mmHg) and the presence/absence of a postural drop
Heart rate and rhythm (clinician-measured sinus tachycardia HR 108, no murmurs/rubs)
Respiratory rate and chest auscultation (clinician-measured RR 22, clear chest)
Abdominal examination (clinician-measured: soft, non-distended, mild voluntary guarding, no true tenderness, rigidity or masses)
Digital rectal examination (deferred by the assessing clinician as history did not suggest a bleed)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: No emergency escalation is required once haematemesis, melena, syncope and ischaemic chest pain are excluded by history: provide reassurance and anxiety de-escalation, arrange routine GP follow-up, and safety-net firmly for the red flags above.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The real risk is directional error either way: OVER-triaging a benign anxiety response to the ED, or UNDER-triaging by dismissing without excluding the GI-bleed/cardiac mimics and without safety-netting.
